ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Khoya Dum Chicken. FOODON_03302757 (chicken dish) is the nearest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Khoya Dum Chicken. A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) is the closest defensible parent. The dish also contains khoya (reduced milk solids) and spices not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Khoya Dum Chicken as a prepared dish.

main_ingredients: whole spices (cardamom, cloves, bay leaf, cinnamon)
ground spices (coriander, cumin, chilli, garam masala)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Khoya Dum Chicken is approximately 350 g, comprising bone-in chicken pieces in a rich khoya-based gravy, served as a main course with bread or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken curry, with bone, cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition); supplemented with khoya (mawa) composition data from the same source for the milk-solid component
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Khoya Dum Chicken is not listed as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken curry (bone-in, cooked) as the primary base, with upward adjustment to fat, saturated fat, and energy to account for the substantial khoya (reduced whole-milk solids) and cashew nut content characteristic of this dish. The adaptation is conservative and consistent with published recipe analyses for Mughlai-style chicken preparations. Confidence is medium due to the absence of an exact match.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat-based curry dishes — analogous estimate based on chicken curry GI data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Khoya Dum Chicken. GI is estimated at 30 based on analogous low-carbohydrate chicken curry dishes reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The dish is predominantly protein and fat with a low carbohydrate content (~5.5 g/100 g), primarily from khoya lactose and spice pastes, supporting a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (30 × 5.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 5.8 g, rounded to one decimal place.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg/100 g; ~1470 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving can contribute approximately 60–70% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (~5.5 g/100 g) and estimated low glycemic index (~30)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Khoya Dum Chicken has a low glycemic index and low carbohydrate load, making it a suitable protein-rich main course for people with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; however, the saturated fat from khoya warrants moderation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from khoya (reduced milk solids) and ghee (~5.2 g saturated fat/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The substantial saturated fat content from khoya and ghee may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to small portions and prefer preparations made with less ghee and reduced-fat dairy alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Khoya Dum Chicken (also known as Khoya Dum Murgh or Mawa Dum Chicken) is a rich North Indian Mughal-inspired chicken dish in which bone-in chicken pieces are slow-cooked using the 'dum' technique — a method of sealing the pot and cooking over low heat to trap steam and intensify flavours. The defining ingredient is khoya (also called mawa), which is whole milk reduced by prolonged simmering until it becomes a dense, fudge-like solid. Khoya imparts a characteristic creamy, slightly sweet richness to the gravy that distinguishes this dish from standard chicken curries. The preparation typically involves marinating chicken in yogurt and spices, sautéing whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf) in ghee, building a base of onion, ginger, garlic, and tomato, incorporating ground spices (coriander, cumin, chilli, garam masala), adding crumbled khoya and cashew paste, and finally slow-cooking the chicken under a sealed lid. The result is a deeply aromatic, velvety gravy with a golden-brown colour. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~14.5 g/100 g) from chicken, moderate in fat (~12 g/100 g) with a notable saturated fat contribution from khoya and ghee, and low in carbohydrates (~5.5 g/100 g), yielding approximately 185 kcal/100 g. The estimated glycemic index is low (~30), making it suitable for glycemic management when served without high-GI staples. Sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant preparations. Clinically, the dish is appropriate for protein-focused meals but requires portion control for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its saturated fat and sodium content. It is naturally gluten-free and commonly prepared halal. In Singapore, it is served at North Indian and Mughlai restaurants.
common_user_queries: What is khoya in chicken curry?
Is Khoya Dum Chicken high in calories?
Can diabetics eat Khoya Dum Chicken?
What are the allergens in Khoya Dum Chicken?
How much protein is in Khoya Dum Chicken?
meal_context: paired with naan, roti, or basmati rice

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Khoya Dum Chicken exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken curry (bone-in, cooked) with upward adjustment for khoya and cashew content. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable low-carbohydrate chicken curry dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation is methodologically grounded in approved sources and the dish's macronutrient profile is well-constrained by its known ingredients.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Chicken curry, with bone, cooked' as the closest approved source match, with adjustments for khoya (mawa) and cashew nut content per ICMR-NIN khoya composition data. An exact laboratory analysis of this specific dish is not available in any approved source. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 5. GI is estimated at 30 (low) based on analogous chicken curry data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI Values; no direct GI measurement for Khoya Dum Chicken was found in any approved source. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 5.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 5.8 g for a 350 g restaurant portion. 7. The dish is served at Indian House / Dumpukht Grills & Curry, Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey data source record GD003442). 8. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context (Indian Muslim / Mughlai cuisine); formal halal certification was not verified from the input data. 9. Multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are provided per Singaporean dish requirements, as this dish is served in Singapore. Chinese names are descriptive translations (奶油焖鸡 = cream braised chicken) as no established Chinese-script name exists for this specific dish.
